step_by_step_thinking:
The mandibular molar typically has four roots, which form distinct tubercles on their crowns. This structure aligns with having multiple tubercules rather than being haplodont (having only one root or crown tubercle). Given that it does not fit triconodont classification as there are more than three cusps/tubercles, quadritubercular accurately describes its characteristic anatomy.

Mandibular molars usually have two distal roots and two mesial roots, each forming distinct tubercles on the tooth surface, making them quintuple-tuberculate but often simplified for educational purposes to quadruple due to overlapping structures.

Mandibular molars usually have two distal roots and two mesial roots, each forming distinct tubercles on the tooth surface, making them quintuple-tuberculate but often simplified for educational purposes to quadruple due to overlapping structures.
